searches.intent: page deeper into effective uhuki awa search
searches.notes: Paged through results 21-100. Many PAGE-type results (full newspaper pages). Article results include legends (Ka-Miki, Pikoiakaalala, Umi), news columns, and a Kamakau history article. Sent promising articles to triage agents.

searches.intent: page deeper into effective awa Hakipuu search
searches.notes: Paged through results 21-60. Found: Ke Aliiwahine circuit article (1929), Makelei legend (1922), Koolau news (1874), 1848 land law. Sent to triage.
kumu means trunk/source/reason; matches everything. Mostly harbor and unrelated articles.

searches.notes: OR-matching too broad; mostly harbor/shipping articles

searches.notes: Broad due to OR matching but top results include: 1838 Lahaina awa law (KKH18381219), 1848 NO KA AWA, 1856/1870/1880 HE KANAWAI. Sent promising law articles to triage.

searches.notes: OR-matching too broad (Kamakau OR awa). Many Ka Moolelo Hawaii series entries but most are about other topics.

searches.intent: Propagation term from Kamakau - stem cuttings
searches.notes: kakiwi means bone/joint broadly, not just awa cuttings. Result #3 is our existing Kamakau article. Most other results are noise (kakiwi in bone/body context).

searches.intent: Find full Kamakau Ka Moolelo Hawaii series
searches.notes: Found the complete series (dozens of installments in Ke Au Okoa, 1869-1871). Checked adjacent installment Helu 9 (Dec 9, 1869) - covers fishponds/canoes/gourds, no awa. Confirms Helu 8 is the awa-specific installment.

searches.notes: 109 results. Mostly mele, mo'olelo, and letters. No obviously cultivation-specific titles.

22292 results. Way too broad. 'ano' too common.
15818 results. 5 OR-matched words = very broad. Harbor/shipping articles dominate.
1121 results. Mostly mele, mo'olelo, kanikau. No obviously cultivation-specific content.
8031 results. Too broad. Some laau lapaau articles but not triaged.
8 results all from Ka Wai Ola (modern). Cultural/commercial articles, not traditional cultivation.

searches.notes: Lanakila = victory, too generic. All results about boat races, elections, legends.

Moving away from district reports. Mostly untitled articles, political articles, gold mining societies.

searches.intent: Young awa shoot - Kamakau propagation term
searches.notes: 214 results. Most 'puawa' uses are in non-awa contexts. Hakipuu article (KNK18770721) was about a money pit, not awa. The term doesn't reliably disambiguate to awa cultivation.
Too broad. kuahiwi and awa match separately. Top results are articles already reviewed.
Surfaced Kamakau (validation). New: Namakaokapaoo legend. Also Kauai travel survey and Ka Elele 1846 opinion piece. Mostly overlapping with known articles.

articles.title: He Moolelo No Ka Awa A Me Kona Wahi I Loaa Mai Ai
articles.author: John Maka
articles.reason: Contains propagation method, variety names, growing regions

articles.title: NO KA AWA. OLELO AE LIKE
articles.reason: The actual 1846 awa prohibition law: specifies max 6 acres per mala, one mala per mokupuni for medicinal use. Provides legal framework for awa cultivation regulation.

articles.title: IE PAIO NO KA AWA
articles.author: J. H. Kaehu
articles.reason: Debate format with economic data: 2-acre awa garden (elua eka ka loa, hookahi eka ka laula) yields $300+. Also describes transport of awa with government permits near Kaneohe.

articles.title: KAPU KA AWA
articles.author: S. W. Maikai
articles.reason: Official notice from Luna kuai Awa o ka Mokupuni Oahu (Awa Sales Officer). Orders ban on planting (aole oukou e kanu iki i ka awa) and uprooting of awa planted after Aug 1846. Mentions kahuna lapaau accept awa as medicinal.

articles.title: HE WAHI MANAO PAIPAI
articles.author: J. H. Kaihalapia
articles.reason: Letter from Kohala confirming awa grew in gardens (mala) across the district. After government-ordered uprooting, awa regrew naturally in people's gardens (ulu wale hou mai kekahi anau kumu awa ma na mala o kanaka).

articles.title: Ka Awa
articles.reason: Social critique mentioning 'mala awa ma kuahiwi o kona aina' (awa garden in mountains of his land), 'kanu malu ia na awa' (awa secretly planted), and landowner ordering awa pulled out. Confirms mountain awa gardens as common.

articles.title: Kaao No Pupualenalena
articles.reason: Legend about dog stealing from chief Hakau's awa garden. Mentions: mala awa on cliff of Puaahuku below Waipio; guards (kanaka kiai) posted at garden; harvesting by pulling (huhuki ana i ka awa); garden nearly depleted by repeated harvesting.

articles.title: E KUIIA AKA A LAWA PONO NA WAHI PANA LEI KAU A-I NO KAHUKU LEWA
articles.reason: Mele/poem containing: 'E kii ko awa i Hakipuu, I ka awa ililena ka uka o Hona' (Get your awa at Hakipuu, the awa ililena of the uplands of Hona). Identifies variety name and specific location.

articles.title: Makemakeia Ka Awa I Laau
articles.reason: Commercial wild harvest info; habitat details (mountain regions)

articles.title: Kaao Hooniua Puuwai No KA-MIKI
articles.reason: Legendary story describing awa ililena growing on ohia tree branches (kamahele); roots that kokolo (crawl/spread). Growth pattern detail in supernatural context.

articles.title: KEIA AO KELA AO
articles.reason: Spiritual fiction; uses scientific name Piper Methysticum. Describes preparation: kumu (trunk) and aa (roots) chewed by teeth or pounded with stones. No cultivation content.

articles.title: NO KA AWA A ME KA HOOMA NAKII
articles.author: M. Kamalanai
articles.reason: About awa prohibition enforcement and kahuna practices in Kalihi. Mentions 'na wahi kanu awa a pau' (all places awa was planted) confirming widespread planting. Enforcement by Paki.

articles.title: E Puna----e! Hoolono Mai!!
articles.reason: Political article; mentions 'kahi o ka awa ililena' as geographic reference for Puna uplands. Confirms awa ililena grows in uplands of Kaliu/Puna.

articles.title: HE HAINA NANE
articles.author: Jos. Nalehuaoawini
articles.reason: Riddle answer citing mele: 'O ka awa ililena i ka uka o Kaliu.' Confirms Kaliu as location for awa ililena variety.

articles.title: NO KA AWA
articles.author: Tito Kalauau
articles.reason: Social/moral critique of drinking; only mentions 'kanuia ka awa mauka' (planted upland)

articles.title: NO KA AWA A ME KA BAKA
articles.author: Haanio
articles.reason: Social critique of awa/tobacco; no cultivation methods

articles.title: NO KA AWA
articles.author: J. H. Kaehu
articles.reason: Social concerns; lists planting locations but no methods

articles.title: No ka Awa
articles.author: Rev. M. Kuaea
articles.reason: Religious/ceremonial critique; no cultivation info

articles.title: Halawai o ka Ahahui mahiai o Kona Akau
articles.reason: Farmers association approved awa planting but no methods discussed

articles.title: KANAWAI NO KE AWA MA LAHAINA, MA KA MOKUPUNI O MAUI
articles.reason: Law regulating foreign ships at the port (awa = harbor) of Lahaina. Not about awa kava at all; the word 'awa' here means harbor/port. No cultivation content.

articles.title: NO KA AWA
articles.author: Kinaiawa
articles.reason: Letter urging Ka Elele to speak against awa. Mentions existence of mala awa (awa gardens) on Oahu/Maui and awa transported to Honolulu, but the content is entirely an exhortation to abandon growing (kanu), selling (kuai), and drinking (inu) awa. No cultivation methods or growing details.

articles.title: NO KA AWA
articles.reason: Political commentary about awa prohibition debate in the legislature (Ahaolelo). Discusses petitions to legalize awa from Oahu, Kauai, and Maui representatives. Entirely about prohibition policy and social harms of awa drinking. No cultivation content whatsoever.

articles.title: HE KANAWAI (E hoololi ana - no ka awa)
articles.reason: Law amending awa statute (approved Sept 16, 1854, published 1856). Pauku 1 explicitly legalizes planting awa ('e hiki no ma ke kanawai, i na kanaka a pau ko kanu i ka awa maloko o keia Aupuni'). Establishes licensed sellers (Luna kuai awa), sets pricing (2/3 to grower, 1/3 to government), restricts consumption to medicinal use with kahuna lapaau prescription. Historically significant for legal framework of awa cultivation, though contains no growing methods.

articles.title: Ka mana o ke Aupuni maluna o ka poe kuai Awa
articles.author: J. Mala [OCR unclear]
articles.reason: Editorial about government authority over awa sellers. Discusses licensing fees by district ($100-$500), debates whether to continue current regulations, fully legalize, or completely prohibit awa. Notes awa is a traditional Hawaiian plant ('he laau kahiko no ia no keia pae aina') and a medicinal plant (laau lapaau). Discusses planting (kanu) and selling in policy context only. No cultivation methods.

articles.title: HE KANAWAI E HOOLOLI ANA I KE KANAWAI E HOOPONOPONO ANA I KE KUAI ANA I KA AWA
articles.reason: Law amending awa sales regulations (approved July 18, 1870). Detailed licensing requirements and fees by district. References 'poe mahi awa' (awa farmers) who may only sell to government agents, and awa as laau lapaau. Regulates the awa cultivation-to-sale chain but provides no information about growing methods, soil, or cultivation techniques.

articles.title: HE KANAWAI E HOAKAKA ANA I KA UKU O NA PAILOTA, MA NA AWA O KAHULUI, MAUI, A ME HILO, HAWAII
articles.reason: Law about pilot fees at harbors (awa = harbor/port) of Kahului and Hilo. The word 'awa' here means harbor, not kava. Entirely about maritime pilot compensation. No relation to awa kava cultivation.

articles.title: KA MOOLELO HAWAII (Helu 8)
articles.author: S. M. Kamakau
articles.reason: Major historical account by Kamakau covering awa cultivation in detail: describes awa varieties and their physical appearance (papa eleele, papakoa, hiwa, moi, makea, mokihana), growing conditions (rainy lands, warm lands, moist slopes, forest edges), planting methods using huli/cuttings with detailed soil preparation (bringing material from shore, composting with opilopilo/opala, letting decompose before planting), propagation through kakiwi (stem cuttings), maturation timeline (2-3 years to harvest), and co-cultivation with wauke and kalo. Also describes harvesting of puawa (young shoots) for propagation.

articles.title: NANI KA AINA HOOPULAPULA O MOLOKAI
articles.author: W. T. Coelho (Kuelo)
articles.reason: Travel report about Molokai homestead lands. 'Awa' appears only in context of harbor/channel (ke awa), not the awa plant. No awa cultivation content whatsoever.

articles.title: Na Hunahuna Anoai no ka Ua Loku mai o Hanalei
articles.author: M. K. Nakula
articles.reason: Regional news from Hanalei covering fishing accidents, tax collection, and local affairs. No awa plant references; 'awa' appears only as 'awapae' (harbor/landing place) and 'awakea' (noon).

articles.title: NA ANOAI
articles.reason: News column with miscellaneous items. 'Awa' appears only as harbor/channel context (ke awa o Honolulu, ke awa o Kapalekua). No reference to the awa plant or cultivation.

articles.title: NU HOU KULOKO
articles.reason: Local news compilation. Awa mentioned only as beverage consumed in a house that subsequently caught fire ('he inu awa ka hana a loko o ua hale la'). No cultivation content.

articles.title: Ka Hoomanamana Akua ma Honaunau
articles.author: David P. Kapua'i
articles.reason: Article about idol worship practices at Honaunau. Awa mentioned only as ritual offering to akua and as beverage ('he awa ke kope e inu ai', 'apuawa'). No cultivation or growing content.

articles.title: No ka awa
articles.reason: Editorial/opinion piece about awa as a traditional plant of Hawaii. Discusses awa's role in Hawaiian medicine (lapaau), argues it is not harmful like distilled alcohol, notes it has spread across Pacific islands. Contains some cultivation-relevant content: mentions awa grew and was cultivated across the islands ('ua hooulu nui ia'), notes it was not 'hoawaawaia' (distilled/processed) unlike foreign spirits. Primarily an advocacy piece but contains general statements about awa cultivation and its importance to mahiai (farmers). Final line references planting: 'he nui no ka poe e kanu ana'.

articles.title: KA HUAKAI KAAPUNI A KE ALIIWAHINE IA OAHU NEI
articles.author: George Pooloa
articles.reason: Travel narrative about Queen Emma's journey around Oahu. Mentions awa only in passing as beverage served at feasts, not cultivation.

articles.title: Makelei, ka Laau Pii Ona a ka I'a o Moaula-Nui-Akea i Kaulana
articles.reason: Mo'olelo (legend/narrative) set in Koolau region. Word 'awa' appears as 'awa aloalo lena...o Kaelepulu' referring to the lagoon/channel, not the awa plant. No cultivation content.

articles.title: Na mea hou o Kapali Koolau, a hiki i kekahi hapa o Waialua
articles.author: S. Waiwaiole
articles.reason: News report about storm/wind damage across Koolau and Waialua regions. Discusses damaged buildings and churches. No mention of awa cultivation whatsoever.

articles.title: HE KANAWAI NO NA AINA PONOI O KA MOI, A ME NA AINA O KE AUPUNI
articles.reason: Government land law listing Crown and Government lands (ahupuaa and ili) across all islands. Word 'awa' only appears in land names like 'Kaawaloa, Awa a me kahi honua i kai'. No awa cultivation content.

articles.title: KELA A ME KEIA
articles.reason: General news miscellany column. Very poor OCR. The word 'awa' appears once likely referring to harbor/port, not the plant. No awa cultivation content.

articles.title: HE MAU HUNAHUNA NO KE KAHUA MAI LEPERA O KALAWAO ME KALAUPAPA
articles.reason: Article about the leprosy settlement at Kalawao/Kalaupapa. Discusses food provisioning and governance. 'Awa' appears only as 'kiai awa' (awa guard) in context of policing contraband. No cultivation content.

articles.title: Halahu mawaho o ke Kanawai a me na Kanawai o keia Aupuni
articles.author: S. K. Kali'a
articles.reason: Article about illegal awa trade/licensing in Kohala Hema. Complains about selling awa without proper license, transporting awa from Waimea to Waipio. Awa as trade good without any growing or cultivation details.

articles.title: KA AWA KAU LAAU O PUNA
articles.reason: OCR completely garbled beyond title. Body text unreadable. Title suggests forest awa of Puna but no usable text present.
articles.series: Possibly part of 'Ka Awa Kau Laau o Puna' series

articles.title: KA AWA ONA KAU LAAU O PUNA
articles.reason: Short advertisement for purchasing awa from Mrs. Ane Kaea at fish market. No cultivation content - awa as trade good only.

articles.title: KA AWA ONA KAU LAAU O PUNA
articles.author: [OCR unclear: possibly HENRY KAI'IA'KOI]
articles.reason: OCR extremely garbled throughout. Body text almost entirely unreadable symbols. Cannot determine if cultivation content exists.
articles.series: Possibly part of 'Ka Awa Kau Laau o Puna' series

articles.title: KE AWA O KAUNALOA MA PUNA
articles.reason: Article about opening awa (harbor/bay) at Kaunaloa for transportation. The word 'awa' here means harbor/channel, not the awa plant.

articles.title: NO KA MAHIAI MA HAMAKUA
articles.author: N. Eeau
articles.reason: Agricultural Society report listing crop acreage across Hamakua districts. Records awa acreage: Honokau 4 eka, Kawela 14 eka, Kapulena 6 eka, total 25 eka. Historical evidence of cultivation scale.

articles.title: Kumumanao a S. P. Ahiona
articles.author: S. P. Ahiona
articles.reason: Political opinion arguing for banning awa licenses, cultivation, sales. Discusses social harms. Mentions 'kanu awa' only to propose prohibition. No cultivation techniques.

articles.title: HE HANA NA NA MAKUA
articles.reason: Short advertisement for Chamberlain's Liniment. Mentions 'koko awa' in medicinal product context. No cultivation content.

articles.title: HE HANA NA NA MAKUA
articles.reason: Advertisement for Chamberlain's Liniment (Benson, Smith & Co.). Nearly identical to KNK19150326-01.2.55. No cultivation content.

articles.title: Ka Mahiai
articles.reason: General farming encouragement about kalo, sugar, oranges, coffee. 'Awa' only appears as 'na awa ku moku' (harbors). No awa plant content.

articles.title: NA LAIKINI AWA
articles.reason: Brief news about auction of awa licenses at Aliiolani Hale. Lists purchasers and prices. Awa trade licensing only.

articles.title: NU KA AHAHUI MAHIAI MA KAU
articles.author: D. F. Sanford (Kakauolelo)
articles.reason: Kau Agricultural Society meeting report. Crops listed: papapa, kulina, huiia, akaakai, uala, alani, liku, kope, maneko. No awa-the-plant. Only 'awa' is harbor reference.

articles.title: KA AHAHUI MAHIAI MA HAMAKUA HAWAII
articles.author: M. K. N.
articles.reason: Letter encouraging papapa (tobacco) farming in Honokaa, Hamakua. No awa-the-plant. Only 'awa' is harbor of Honolulu.

articles.title: NA AHAHUI MAHIAI O KANAKA MAOLI MA HAWAII
articles.reason: Overview of Native Hawaiian Agricultural Societies forming across Hawaii. No crop data, no awa mention.

articles.title: Ahahui Mahiai o Waioli, Kauai
articles.reason: Founding constitution of Waioli Agricultural Society (Hanalei/Anahola, Kauai). Proposes rice and cotton. No awa mention despite being in a historically awa-rich region.

articles.title: Ahahui Mahiai ma Molokai
articles.reason: Molokai Agricultural Society report from Kalaupapa. Crops: uala, ipupu, ipu haole, kulina. No awa mention.

articles.title: NO KA MAHIAI MA LAHAINA
articles.author: Ioane Richardson
articles.reason: General farming commentary at Lahaina. Discusses uala, maia. No awa reference.

articles.title: HANA, APANA 4, MOKUPUNI O MAUI
articles.author: M. Kahananui
articles.reason: Ahahui Mahiai bylaws for Hana district. Crops listed: kalo, uala, maia, kulina, papapa, alani, kope. No awa.

articles.title: He olelo paipai a Mose Kapihe inua o ka Ahahui Mahiai o Makawao
articles.author: M. Kapihe
articles.reason: Motivational speech about farming diligence to Makawao Agricultural Society. No awa mention.

articles.title: No ka halawai Ahahui mahiai Pulupulu ma Kumunui Kaupo
articles.author: T. C. Wilmington
articles.reason: Cotton agricultural society meeting at Kaupo, Maui. Entirely about pulupulu (cotton) cultivation. No awa reference.

articles.title: Halawai o ka ahahui mahiai o Kona Akau
articles.author: G. W. Pilipo
articles.reason: North Kona Agricultural Society meeting. Explicitly resolves to appoint committee for 'ke kanu awa e kuai' (planting awa for sale). Documents formal consideration of commercial awa cultivation in 1862.

articles.title: Kumumanao a S. P. Ahiona (April 16, 1870)
articles.author: S. P. Ahiona
articles.reason: Ahiona Kumumanao series continuation. This installment is entirely about opium licensing, not awa. The series covers various social issues; only the April 9 installment discussed awa.

articles.title: AOLE MAKEHEWA KA HANA
articles.reason: Farming editorial encouraging cultivation of oranges, coffee, sweet potatoes, beans. No awa content at all. Only 'awa' is harbor of Honolulu.

articles.title: KELA MEA KEIA MEA
articles.reason: Miscellaneous news column. About a steamship in Honolulu harbor and California sweet potatoes. 'Awa' = harbor only.

articles.title: NA PALAPALA I KE KUOKOA - He Lua Dala ma Hakipuu Koolaupoko
articles.author: B.E.K.
articles.reason: Letter about a money/treasure pit excavation at Hakipuu. 'Puawa' used as a geographic landmark, not awa plant. No farming content.

articles.title: HE MOOLELO KAAO NO Namakaokapaoo
articles.author: John K. Hiihin
articles.reason: Legend about warrior child at Waiohuli, Kula (Maui). Contains explicit 'mala awa' (awa garden) alongside uala, maia, ko. Awa described as tall as trees. Harvesting by uhuki. New Maui growing region confirmed.

articles.title: Nau na GELINA
articles.author: Gelina
articles.reason: Prohibition debate opinion. Mentions 'na mala awa e mahiia a e kanuia' (awa gardens cultivated and planted) at Kapaau but provides no methods. Argues for full ban.

findings.quote: poke ae no i ka lala a hoolei ilu iloko o ka lua opu awa i hahuki ia ae ai a kanu a paa a omaku ae
findings.note: Propagation method: when harvesting, break off branch and replant in hole

findings.quote: kolo ke aa
findings.note: Roots spread/crawl - describes root growth habit

findings.quote: ka Papa, Alikea ame ka Mokihana; he keokeo ko lakou lala
findings.note: Three varieties named: Papa, Alikea, Mokihana - noted as having white stems

findings.quote: Aia ma Kamaile i Kauai, Halawa i Oahu, ma Maui
findings.note: Named growing regions across islands

findings.quote: wahi pana ma Hakipuu ma Oahu nei
findings.note: Hakipuu on Oahu noted as famous place for awa (wahi pana)

findings.quote: he mau haaeri ka nui o na tona awa o ulu la ma ke kuahiwi ma kahi i kapaia o Awini
findings.note: Hundreds of tons of awa growing wild in mountains at Awini, Kohala

findings.quote: ma ke kuahiwi
findings.note: Awa grows in mountain regions (kuahiwi); terrain too rough for pack animals

findings.quote: Ma ka Mokupuni o Hawaii, hookahi mala wale no e ae ia... Aole eoi aku ka nui o ka mala hookahi mamua o eono eka
findings.note: 1846 law: One mala awa permitted per island for medicinal use, max 6 acres each. Establishes awa was cultivated in formal gardens large enough to require regulation.

findings.quote: Ina e kanu ika mala awa a nui loa, elua eka ka loa; hookahi eka ka laula, iloko oia mala, ua loaa mai na dala $300 a keu aku
findings.note: Economic data: a 2-acre awa garden (2 eka long, 1 eka wide) yields $300+. Shows awa farming was commercially viable.

findings.quote: ua pau ka awa i ka uhukiia ma na mala a pau o Kohala nei... Mahope ulu wale hou mai kekahi anau kumu awa ma na mala o kanaka
findings.note: After ALL awa pulled from Kohala gardens, it regrew on its own in people's gardens. Shows awa resilience and natural regeneration ability.

findings.quote: ua loaa iaia he mala awa ma kuahiwi o kona aina; ua kanu malu ia na awa
findings.note: Awa gardens located in mountain areas (kuahiwi); sometimes planted secretly (kanu malu) due to prohibition.

findings.quote: ua mala awa nei i ka pali o Puaahuku olalo iho... hoonoho aku la i mau kanaka kiai
findings.note: Legend: Chief Hakau's awa garden on cliff of Puaahuku below Waipio. Garden had guards (kanaka kiai) posted. Harvesting by pulling (huhuki).

findings.quote: E kii ko awa i Hakipuu, I ka awa ililena ka uka o Hona
findings.note: Variety 'awa ililena' specifically at Hakipuu, in the uplands of Hona. Corroborates John Maka's Hakipuu reference.

findings.quote: ka awa ililena e ulu ana iluna o ka lala kamahele o Ohia-nui-moe-awakea... o ka awa hoi nona ke aa e kokolo ana a wili i ka honua
findings.note: Ka-Miki legend: awa ililena growing on ohia tree branches; roots that crawl/spread (kokolo) and wrap around earth. Suggests wild awa's epiphytic/forest growth habit.

findings.quote: aole oukou e kanu iki i ka awa... O ka Awa i kanuia mahope mai o ka la 6, o Augate 1846. E uhuki wale ia kela awa
findings.note: Official order: no planting of awa permitted; any awa planted after Aug 6, 1846 to be pulled out. From the Luna kuai Awa o ka Mokupuni Oahu.

findings.quote: e hiki no ma ke kanawai, i na kanaka a pau ko kanu i ka awa maloko o keia Aupuni
findings.note: 1854 law (Pauku 1) legalizing awa planting for all people in the kingdom. Establishes government-licensed sellers with 2/3 payment to grower and 1/3 to government. Consumption restricted to medicinal use with kahuna lapaau prescription. Significant for understanding the legal context in which awa cultivation operated.

findings.quote: elua hapakolu e haawiia ika mea nana ka awa, a hookahi hapakolu o Ilo no ke Aupuni
findings.note: Pricing structure: two-thirds of sale price goes to the awa grower, one-third to the government. Relevant to understanding economic incentives for awa cultivation.

findings.quote: 0 ka awa krkahi ni linnohano a ka mahiai, a ua maikai konu helehelena i ka nana akt>, o kona lau pohepohe maikai, a o kona hakai a me kona mau ponnpona akaaka
findings.note: Kamakau describes awa as valued by the mahiai (farmer), with attractive appearance - round leaves (lau pohepohe), branching stalks (hakai) and joints (ponapona).

findings.quote: 0 ka pnpa eleele a mo ka papokoa, oia na awa nenee haaheo o kona uln ana tne ka hanohano, a o ka liiwa a me ka moi, ho newa kona hukni, akn, ua like kona ponapona ine ka puna ko honuoula, a o ka makea, ua like kona hakai mo ko ka oho, a mo kea hauliuli, ao ka mokihana, ua like kona hakai me ke opu ohe
findings.note: Names several awa varieties with physical descriptions: papa eleele, papakoa (tall/proud growth), hiwa, moi (reddish joints), makea (hakai like hair, bluish), mokihana (hakai like bamboo belly, clustered branching). Descriptions are of plant morphology.

findings.quote: Ua knpono ka ulu o ka awa ma na aina ua noi, a ua knpono no ma na aina mehana, a ua ulu ko awa ma na mukaili hanono wai, a ua ulu raa na lae laau a ma kukaepuaa, a mu ka omau ma na aina ua nni.
findings.note: Describes ideal growing conditions: awa grows well in rainy lands (aina ua nui) and warm lands (aina mehana), on moist slopes that channel water (makaili hanono wai), at forest edges (lae laau), at kukaepuaa, and in sheltered/consistent areas (omau) with rain.

findings.quote: Mai ka Wa kahiko mai ua kanlana na wahi maikai a me ka ona o ka awa, o K'oukou ko Kauai, o Hena ko o o Lanakila ko Maui, o Pnna ko Hawaii.
findings.note: Names famous regional awa-growing areas since ancient times: Koukou on Kauai, Hena on [OCR unclear - possibly Oahu or Molokai], Lanakila on Maui, Puna on Hawaii. These were known for quality and potency (ona) of awa.

findings.quote: Ma kahi ō knnu ai ka wauke, ke kalo maloo, o ka awa pu kekahi e kanu paalaila, o ka maia a me ku .ko, he. mau mea haaheokeia na ke kanaka mahiai. He leanu mala kekahi awa,.a he kanu liilii walo no ka nui i ka wa kahiko, ma na kuauna 6 lea maln kalo, a pela ka mala wauke.
findings.note: Awa was co-cultivated with wauke, dryland kalo, maia (banana), and ko (sugarcane) on the same land. Some awa was planted in its own garden (mala), but most was planted in small amounts on kuauna (borders/edges) of kalo gardens and wauke plots.

findings.quote: No ko kanu ow». Kii mua ko kanōka awa i kahakai, hailini mua a nui, amo a hiki i kahi kupono, alaila, poke mo ka malama i ka puupuu o ka akankn, alaila, hoo-, waa a kipulu i ka lepo opilopilo a me ka opula
findings.note: Detailed planting method: First, the awa farmer goes to the shore (kahakai) to collect material (possibly seaweed/organic matter - hailini), carries it to the planting site, carefully protects the node/joint of the cutting (puupuu o ka akanka), then composts/decomposes it with rich soil (lepo opilopilo) and organic waste (opala).

findings.quote: 110 oliia a okolu makahiki iilaila oo kn n\v:i
findings.note: States awa reaches maturity (oo) in 2-3 years (elua a ekolu makahiki). OCR is garbled but the timeline is discernible.

findings.quote: I ka wu c uui ai k« pnawn, Hlmla, kii k»> kniuvkiv mnhiiii, o knkiwi i ka H:\kin o ka awn, n i k;\ «· n\m\ii tii k:\ pn:\\v:\, nUila, t - lilo no hoi nn kakiwi i man pmiwn hon nku
findings.note: Describes propagation cycle: when puawa (young shoots) emerge, the farmer takes kakiwi (stem cuttings) from the base of the awa plant to create new puawa plantings. The kakiwi become new puawa. OCR is heavily garbled but the propagation cycle from kakiwi to puawa is clear.

findings.quote: o ka ow», aole wule t o ia i ulu ma keia pae inoko pouoi o kakou nei ; me he mea la, uu lnha a ua hooulu nui in do la niea : iiim iin pae moku lehulehu wale o ka mouna Pakipika nei
findings.note: States awa does not only grow in Hawaii but has spread and been cultivated (hooulu nui ia) across many island groups of the Pacific. Emphasizes it was intentionally cultivated (hooulu), not just gathered wild.

findings.quote: O ka awa, aolo i hooinnkankau hoawaawaia e like me ku ua wai ikaika, he muu la okon. a he mau pule pnhu e hoomakaukiiuia nr> aku, m ii Joko maoli mai no o ka lepo ka awa, h mum'i ia iho lu n inu ia
findings.note: Describes that awa is not distilled/processed like strong spirits (wai ikaika). It is prepared from the root (lepo ka awa) directly - chewed (mumi ia) and drunk without being mixed with other substances. Notes preparation takes weeks (mau pule).

findings.quote: eka awa 4
findings.note: Honokau district: 4 eka (acres) of awa among crops including kope 2,146, alani 62, kalo 23

findings.quote: eka awa 14
findings.note: Kawela district: 14 eka of awa -- largest awa acreage of any Hamakua district

findings.quote: eka awa 6
findings.note: Kapulena district: 6 eka of awa alongside kalo 172, uala 140, wauke 10

findings.quote: na eka awa 25
findings.note: Total awa acreage for all Hamakua: 25 eka. With total kope 23,714 and alani 935. 1857 agricultural census.

findings.quote: eka awa l
findings.note: Kaolie district: appears to list 1 eka of awa (OCR 'l' likely = 1)

findings.quote: o ka awa hol kela, he like pu no ke kiekie o ka awa me ka laau, pau iaia nei ika ka ulupa ia, a i ua keiki nei iloko o ka mala awa
findings.note: Namakaokapaoo legend (Kula, Maui): Awa garden described at Waiohuli. Awa grows as tall as trees ('he like pu no ke kiekie o ka awa me ka laau'). Co-cultivated alongside uala, maia, ko on a large farm (mahinaai nui). Harvested by uhuki (pulling). Confirms Maui awa cultivation.

findings.quote: Hooholo ia eka hope Peresidena e koho ia i komite wae no ka noonoo ana i ka pono o ke puhi rama, ame ke kanu awa e kuai, ame ke kanu waina a kaomi waina
findings.note: North Kona Agricultural Society (Feb 25, 1862) formally resolved to appoint committee (Nakapalani, J.W. Makanoanoa, J.H. Kamalo) to evaluate planting awa for commercial sale. Awa grouped with rum and wine as commercial agricultural ventures. Documents institutional consideration of awa as cash crop.

followups.notes: Search for 'John Maka' returned 8,054 results (too broad). Need to search within Ke Alakai o Hawaii specifically, or try different approach. Still worth pursuing.
Searched (214 results). puawa used as: ritual offering (1884), awa portion for sale (1866), legal evidence (1893), tree/landmark (1877). NOT a propagation-specific term. Commerce context only.

Maui mentioned in John Maka article and 1846 law but no Maui-specific awa articles found yet. Session 3: searched 'awa Maui' (9003 results, too broad). Found 1838 KANAWAI NO KE AWA MA LAHAINA which IS Maui-specific (sent to triage). Also 'kanawai awa' surfaced law articles.
Session 2 successfully found 1846-1849 prohibition-era articles with substantial cultivation context. These are the richest pre-1900 sources. The 1846 law and 1847 debate provide quantitative data.

Session 3: Found S. M. Kamakau (KAO18691202-01.2.2, 1869) as major traditional voice with detailed awa cultivation knowledge. Still worth seeking additional practitioner perspectives beyond Kamakau and John Maka.
Searched (321 results). No cultivation-specific content found in top results. Legends and general articles only.
Found Pupualenalena legend (1935) from same newspaper with awa garden details. Newspaper also published from Hilo area.
Searched as 'ka awa laau' (9,925 results) - too broad. OR-matching makes disambiguation impossible with this term.
Searched (14 results). Confirmed: variety associated with Puna uplands (Kaliu), Hakipuu (Hona uplands). Appears in riddles, mele, and Ka-Miki legend. Growth pattern: on ohia trees, crawling roots.
Very effective search (177 results). The harvesting/uprooting term surfaces prohibition-era articles rich in cultivation context. Best disambiguation strategy found.
Director of Limahuli Garden, Kauai. Wrote master's thesis on awa (mentioned in 2006 Ka Wai Ola article). Academic source outside newspaper collection.
Searched (6 results). Only 1900 spiritual fiction and modern articles. No cultivation content, but confirms botanical awareness in Hawaiian-language press.

The 1854 law legalizing awa planting may have prompted articles discussing actual cultivation practices in the years following. Searching for articles about awa planting in the 1854-1860 period could yield cultivation details. Session 3: searched but too broad.

The 1870 law uses the specific term 'poe mahi awa' (awa farmers) as a distinct class of people. This term may appear in other articles that discuss what awa farmers actually did. Session 4: searched page 2 (s047). No cultivation articles found. Ka Mahiai article about farming doesn't mention awa plant.

followups.value: KA MOOLELO HAWAII by S. M. Kamakau - Helu 8 is part of a longer series
followups.notes: Article ends with '(Aole i pau)' indicating continuation. The series covers multiple agricultural topics (wauke cultivation, olona cultivation, fishpond construction also in this installment). Earlier and later installments may have more awa or related cultivation detail. Checked Helu 9 (Dec 9, 1869) - covers fishponds, canoes, gourds. No more awa in adjacent installments.

Kamakau is the most detailed source found so far on traditional Hawaiian awa cultivation methods. His other historical writings in Ke Au Okoa and Ka Nupepa Kuokoa likely contain additional agricultural knowledge. Searched Kamakau awa (733 results, too broad) and checked Ka Moolelo Hawaii series (964 results). Adjacent installments do not cover awa.

Kamakau uses 'kakiwi' as a specific term for awa propagation cuttings. Searching this term may find other articles discussing awa propagation techniques. Searched kakiwi awa (44 results). kakiwi used broadly for bone/joint; not specific enough to surface other awa propagation articles.

followups.value: Koukou (Kauai), Hena, Lanakila (Maui), Puna (Hawaii) - famous awa regions named by Kamakau
followups.notes: Kamakau names these as renowned awa-growing regions from ancient times. Searching these place names with awa may yield region-specific cultivation details. Session 4: searched Koukou awa (s044, 109 results, no cultivation), awa Puna (s045, 4416 results, garbled OCR on promising titles). Found KAA19111202 and KAA19111118 with 'Ka Awa Kau Laau o Puna' titles but OCR completely unreadable - need PDF.

Article discusses awa licensing in Kohala/Waipio. Other articles about awa trade regulation in this region might mention growing conditions or sourcing practices. Session 4: searched laikini awa (s050, 2171 results). All license auction/trade articles. No cultivation content.

followups.value: Download PDF for KAA19111202-01.2.24 and KAA19111118-01.2.30 -- garbled OCR may hide valuable content about forest awa of Puna
followups.notes: Both Ke Aloha Aina Nov-Dec 1911 articles have completely failed OCR. Title 'kau laau' (forest/wild) + 'awa' + 'Puna' suggests content about wild-growing awa in Puna forests.

followups.value: N. Eeau - VP of Ahahui Mahiai o Hamakua
followups.notes: Author of agricultural census. May have other farming reports.

followups.value: Ahahui Mahiai - agricultural society reports from other districts
followups.notes: Agricultural society reports from other districts may contain similar awa acreage data. Session 5: Searched "Ahahui Mahiai" (s057, 2563 results), "Ahahui Mahiai awa" (s060, 1161 results). Triaged 10 agricultural society reports from Kau, Hamakua, Hawaii overview, Waioli/Kauai, Molokai, Lahaina, Hana, Makawao, Kaupo, Kona Akau. Only Kona Akau mentioned awa (commercial planting committee). Other societies focused on cash crops (tobacco, cotton, rice, sweet potatoes).

followups.value: Ka Awa Kau Laau o Puna - possible multi-part series in Ke Aloha Aina Nov-Dec 1911
followups.notes: Two articles with similar titles 2 weeks apart suggest a series. Need PDF scans to read actual content.

Author of anti-awa opinion piece ending with '(Aole i pau)'. Continuation might contain more detail about awa farming practices the author wants banned. Session 5: Searched "S. P. Ahiona" (s063, 171 results). Found 7+ Kumumanao installments (March-April 1870). The April 16 installment is entirely about opium licensing. The series covers various social issues; only April 9 discussed awa. Dead end for awa cultivation.

followups.value: laikini awa returns trade/regulatory articles not cultivation - low value term
followups.notes: Mark as low-value term to avoid in future cultivation-focused searches.

followups.value: Subsequent Ahahui Mahiai o Kona Akau meetings after March 1862 -- look for committee report on awa planting
followups.notes: Committee of Nakapalani, Makanoanoa, and Kamalo tasked with evaluating awa planting for sale. A follow-up report would document their findings. Search Ka Hoku o ka Pakipika mid-1862 onward.

followups.value: Ahahui Mahiai o Waioli subsequent reports -- founding article (1861) only has bylaws, later meetings may include awa data
followups.notes: Hanalei/Anahola region historically associated with awa. Founding doc proposes rice and cotton but later reports could mention awa.

followups.value: Namakaokapaoo legend continuation (Aole i pau) -- may contain more awa garden details from Kula/Maui
followups.notes: Legend about warrior child destroying farm at Waiohuli. Story continues. Later installments may describe awa garden restoration or more cultivation details.
